dcterms_abstract: Dr. H. Richard Adams, Dean of Veterinary Medicine at Texas A&M University, stands at podium to speak about the cloned bull project to the press. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University stand to the side of the podium. The latter pair have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: Dr. Albrecht Konrad Eugster making adjustments on a transmission electron microscope, circa 1970's. Albrecht Konrad Eugster began working for Texas A&M as the head of Diagnostic Microbiology for the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL). During his ten-year tenure in this position, he discovered several diseases new to Texas and in some cases, new to the world. Dr. Eugster was the first to isolate canine parvovirus II and published the first report on this disease in world literature. He also found several pathogenic organisms for the first time in Texas that caused several infections in animals. In 1980 he was appointed by the Texas A&M University System Board of Regents as Executive Director of TVMDL and later received the additional title of Associate Vice Chancellor for Agriculture. In 2001 Dr. Eugster retired and was appointed Director Emeritus by the Texas A&M System Board of Regents.

dcterms_abstract: Three women sit at desks while Dr. Lelve Gayle stands next to the woman in the middle desk and looks at something on her desk. Dr. Gayle earned his doctorate in veterinary medicine in 1964 and Master of Science degree in veterinary toxicology in 1980, both from Texas A&M University. Prior to joining the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL) in 1976, he worked in veterinary private practice and for the U. S. Air Force. Dr. Gayle was president of the Texas Academy of Veterinary Practice in 1985 and president of the Texas Veterinary Medical Association in 1996. He worked at the TVMDL for 31 years and was executive director from 2002 until July 31, 2007 when he retired. On August 6th, 2017, Dr. Lelve Gayle passed from this life at the age of 77.

dc_title: John August, Department Head, Veterinary Small Animal Medicine and Surgery
dcterms_abstract: A faculty photograph of the Head of the Department for Veterinary Small Animal Medicine and Surgery. Dr. John R. August is a native of Surrey, England. He received his pre-veterinary education at Eastbourne College in Sussex, England. In 1973, he graduated with honors from the Royal Veterinary College at the University of London, and became a member of the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons. Following an internship and residency in small animal internal medicine, he remained on the faculty at Auburn University as an Assistant Professor until 1982. While at Auburn, he was awarded the Master of Science degree in 1977, and became a diplomate of the American College of Veterinary Internal Medicine (Specialty of Internal Medicine) in 1979. He joined the faculty of the Virginia-Maryland Regional College of Veterinary Medicine as Associate Professor and Coordinator of Medical Services in 1982, and was appointed Professor of Companion Animal Medicine in 1986. From 1986 until 1997, he was Professor and Head of the Department of Small Animal Medicine and Surgery at Texas A&M University, and served as Deputy Dean of the College of Veterinary Medicine. He received the Texas A&M University Association of Former Students Faculty Distinguished Achievement Award in Administration in 1994. He has also served as Interim Head of the Department of Veterinary Pathobiology. In 2014, he was appointed Associate Dean of Faculties and Director of the Center for Teaching Excellence at Texas A&M University. His professional interests include feline internal medicine, distance education, and the effective use of educational technology in the classroom to promote learning. Dr. August shared responsibility for the feline internal medicine service in the Veterinary Medical Teaching Hospital for 15 years. In 2014, he was selected as the Evelyn Williams Visiting Professor at the Faculty of Veterinary Science, University of Sydney. He was the editor-in-chief of Consultations in Feline Internal Medicine, volumes 1 through 6, published by Saunders Elsevier. Volume 6 was published in 2010. On February 12, 2016 he returned to College Station to accept an appointment of Dean of Faculties and Associate Provost at Texas A&M University.

dcterms_abstract: Dr. Albrecht Konrad Eugster sitting at a transmission electron Microscope, circa 1970's. Albrecht Konrad Eugster began working for Texas A&M as the head of Diagnostic Microbiology for the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL). During his ten year tenure in this position, he discovered several diseases new to Texas and in some cases, new to the world. Dr. Eugster was the first to isolate canine parvovirus II and published the first report on this disease in world literature. He also found several pathogenic organisms for the first time in Texas that caused several infections in animals. In 1980 he was appointed by the Texas A&M University System Board of Regents as Executive Director of TVMDl and later received the additional title of Associate Vice Chancellor for Agriculture. In 2001 Dr. Eugster retired and was appointed Director Emeritus by the Texas A&M System Board of Regents.

dcterms_abstract: Dr. Lelve G. Gayle between 1977 and 1978, talking on the phone at a desk while he reviews a file. Dr. Gayle earned his doctorate in veterinary medicine in 1964 and Master of Science in veterinary toxicology in 1980, both from Texas A&M University. Prior to joining the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL) in 1976, he worked in a veterinary private practice and for the U. S. Air Force. Dr. Gayle was president of the Texas Academy of Veterinary Practice in 1985 and president of the Texas Veterinary Medical Association in 1996. He worked at the TVMDL for 31 years and was executive director from 2002 until July 31, 2007 when he retired. On August 6th, 2017, Dr. Lelve Gayle passed from this life at the age of 77.

Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: Two men box up the remains of a dead calf to be sent to the Texas A&M Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory for analysis. It is thought that the man on the left may possibly be Roy Herrmann, a rancher from Caldwell, Texas, who served as president of the Texas Hereford Association from 1975 to 1976. The man on the right is believed to be R. W. Sprowls, a pathologist from the Texas A&M Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory who responded to calls from animal owners and veterinarians treating both large and small animals. Starting his career as a pathologist, Dr. Sprowls has been in leadership positions at the Amarillo lab since 1975. He is nationally renowned as an astute diagnostician, helping to earn the lab's reputation as the premier U.S. facility for feedlot disease diagnostics. . His innovative approaches include promoting batch/pooled testing of clinical specimens for reproductive diseases such as Trichomoniasis. Using this method, beef producers have controlled the disease and saved 75 to 80 percent in costs over individual testing. Working with a range of clients that include some of the world's largest feedlots. He was instrumental in the development and success of the Academy of Veterinary Consultants, now a 900-member national group of beef cattle veterinarians and received the 2013 Diagnostic Services Award in Excellence from Texas A&M AgriLife.

dcterms_abstract: Stainless steel diagnostic table with multiple bagged specimens and a box labeled 'Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Lab Biological Specimen RUSH.' The first Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratories facility was built in 1969, and since has had three expansions, in 1978, 1992 and 2004. The building shown in this image is the 1992 construction of added space for the laboratories. As of 2014 it was reported that the diagnostic Laboratories handles more than 825,000 tests per year from clients in Texas, the United States and around the world, and the College Station facilities employs approximately 100 staff and serves as one of the two full-service facilities in the TVMDL agency. The other full service Laboratory is in Amarillo, with additional poultry diagnostic laboratories located in Center and Gonzales. A new facility for TVMDL was completed in College Station in 2017.

dcterms_abstract: Ralph Fisher pets 'Second Chance' and the bull leans into Mr. Fisher. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: A woman evaluating data that is being collected by an SMA 12/60 dialyzer in a pathology lab. dc_title: Aerial Image of Veterinary College and Surrounding Areas, number 67
dcterms_abstract: This image shows many of the facilities that made up the Veterinary College's Complex circa 1969.  The back of this photograph is also pictured with a key to the names of the buildings.  Building A is facing Highway 60 (University Drive) and is the long building with many windows in the lower right of the picture. Building B is the next structure on the left facing the road, and Building C is the to the far left.  All three of these structures are linked by corridors. The empty lot located behind buildings B and A is the future site of the first State Diagnostic Laboratory. Building E is to the right of the vacant lot. All the other buildings are located on the land behind the main buildings A-C. Building I  can be identified by its center classic big barn structure with long stable wings to the right and left, shown just left and slightly above center in the picture.

It is thought that the man on the left may possibly be Roy Herrmann, a rancher from Caldwell, Texas, who served as president of the Texas Hereford Association from 1975 to 1976. The man on the right is believed to be R. W. Sprowls, a pathologist from the Texas A&M Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory who responded to calls from animal owners and veterinarians treating both large and small animals. Starting his career as a pathologist, Dr. Sprowls has been in leadership positions at the Amarillo lab since 1975. He is nationally renowned as an astute diagnostician, helping to earn the lab's reputation as the premier U.S. facility for feedlot disease diagnostics. . His innovative approaches include promoting batch/pooled testing of clinical specimens for reproductive diseases such as Trichomoniasis. Using this method, beef producers have controlled the disease and saved 75 to 80 percent in costs over individual testing. Working with a range of clients that include some of the world's largest feedlots. He was instrumental in the development and success of the Academy of Veterinary Consultants, now a 900-member national group of beef cattle veterinarians and received the 2013 Diagnostic Services Award in Excellence from Texas A&M AgriLife.

dcterms_abstract: The original Civil War era photo of the Congregational church in Shandon, Ohio. It is noted that historically, the predominant Welsh community originally known as New London was renamed Shandon, and also referred by locals as "Paddy's Run" Ohio. The building of the Congregational Church of Shandon, Ohio in Butler County was completed in 1856 and the bell was hung in the steeple in 1865. This is the church that Mark Francis, the first dean of the veterinary college at College Station, Texas, attended as a young boy, where his ancestors worshipped and in 1890, where he married Anna Scott Jones. Their son Bebb served with the American Expeditionary Force in France during the first World War and was wounded in action. This is also the church where the marriage of John Albert Scott, the younger brother of Anna Scott Jones, born in Shandon in 1855, took place. Both were descended from James and Anna C. (Jones) Scott. James Scott represented his native state as a valiant soldier of the Union in the Civil War, and he was twenty-one years of age when he established his residence at Shandon, Butler County, where he engaged in the work of his trade, that of shoemaker. There his marriage was solemnized, and five years later he and his wife removed to the farm now owned and operated by their only son. Mrs. Anna C. (Jones) Scott was a daughter of John C. and Jane Jones, both of whom were born in Wales. Mr. Jones numbered among the seven persons who met a tragic death in the collapse of the Congregational church building at Shandon, many years ago.
dcterms_provenance: This nineteenth century photograph is an "Ebay" purchase made by librarian Trenton Boyd and donated to the Medical Sciences Library.

dcterms_abstract: Dr. H. Richard Adams, Dean of Veterinary Medicine at Texas A&M University, stands at podium to speak about the cloned bull project to the press. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University stand to the side of the podium. The latter pair have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: Dr. Albrecht Konrad Eugster sitting at a transmission electron Microscope, circa 1970's. Albrecht Konrad Eugster began working for Texas A&M as the head of Diagnostic Microbiology for the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL). During his ten-year tenure in this position, he discovered several diseases new to Texas and in some cases, new to the world. Dr. Eugster was the first to isolate canine parvovirus II and published the first report on this disease in world literature. He also found several pathogenic organisms for the first time in Texas that caused several infections in animals. In 1980 he was appointed by the Texas A&M University System Board of Regents as Executive Director of TVMDL and later received the additional title of Associate Vice Chancellor for Agriculture. In 2001 Dr. Eugster retired and was appointed Director Emeritus by the Texas A&M System Board of Regents.

dcterms_abstract: Ralph and Sandra Fisher, owners of the cloned bull Second Chance, speak at the press conference for him. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dc_title: Two Men with a Dead Calf, number 3
dcterms_abstract: Two men in a field observe a calf that has died. It is thought that the man on the left may possibly be Roy Herrmann, a rancher from Caldwell, Texas, who served as president of the Texas Hereford Association from 1975 to 1976. The man on the right is believed to be R. W. Sprowls, a pathologist from the Texas A&M Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory who responded to calls from animal owners and veterinarians treating both large and small animals. Starting his career as a pathologist, Dr. Sprowls has been in leadership positions at the Amarillo lab since 1975. He is nationally renowned as an astute diagnostician, helping to earn the lab's reputation as the premier U.S. facility for feedlot disease diagnostics. . His innovative approaches include promoting batch/pooled testing of clinical specimens for reproductive diseases such as Trichomoniasis. Using this method, beef producers have controlled the disease and saved 75 to 80 percent in costs over individual testing. Working with a range of clients that include some of the world's largest feedlots. He was instrumental in the development and success of the Academy of Veterinary Consultants, now a 900-member national group of beef cattle veterinarians and received the 2013 Diagnostic Services Award in Excellence from Texas A&M AgriLife.

dc_title: Home of the Grandparents of Mark Francis
dcterms_abstract: Original photo of the home of William and Mary (Bebb) Vaughan, the daughter of Edward and Margaret Bebb. They were the grandparents of famous College Station Texas A&M professor Mark Francis. William Vaughan was also the first postmaster of Paddy's Run. The Vaughan House or the John Vaughan House, also known as the Vaughan-Francis House, 3756 Hamilton-New London Road, Shandon, was built between 1814-1815 and was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1975. It is noted that historically, the predominant Welsh community, originally known as New London was renamed Shandon, and also referred by locals as "Paddy's Run" Ohio. In 1824, William Vaughan became the first postmaster of Paddy's Run, and the house served as the post office from 1831 to 1847. Martha Ann Vaughan (1832-1905), the daughter of William and Mary Vaughan, married Abner Francis (1829-1894) in 1852. He was the grandson of David Francis (1772-1848) and Mary Rowland (1774-1852) who were among the first group of Welsh settlers to settle Shandon in 1795. Abner Francis was the father of Mark Francis, who was the first Dean of what is now known as Texas A&M University, College of Veterinary Medicine and Biomedical Sciences.
dcterms_provenance: This nineteenth century photograph is an "Ebay" purchase made by librarian Trenton Boyd and donated to the Medical Sciences Library.

dcterms_abstract: Dr. Don Farrell (left) and Dr. Joseph Milton Farrell Jr. (right), veterinarians at their newly opened animal hospital in Arlington, Texas. The two brothers opened their veterinary practice together on August 9th, 1948. Their father Dr. J. M. Farrell Sr., the city meat inspector for the city of Fort Worth, was also a practicing veterinarian when the boys were young and inspired them to one day want to open their own clinic. Dr. Joseph Farrell graduated from Texas A&M Veterinary School in 1944 and spent three and a half years with the Veterinary Corps of the Army, being discharged in January, 1947. In June, 1948 his brother Don, also graduated from Texas A&M with his D.V.M. holding a reserve commission in the Army.

dcterms_abstract: Image of 'Second Chance' with researchers Dr. Jonathan Hill (far left) and Dr. Mark Westhusin (far right) and the owners of 'Chance', the cloned bull, Ralph and Sandra Fisher. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: Portrait of Theobald Smith from 1889. Smith was a faculty member in the microbiology department of the Columbian University of Washington now known as George Washington University. It was Dr. Theobald Smith's investigation and reports about ticks that aided Dr. Francis in his research into how to eradicate tick fever in cattle. After graduating from Albany Medical College, Dr. Smith became a research lab assistant with the Veterinary Division of the U.S. Department of Agriculture in Washington, D.C. Smith turned his attention to Texas fever, a debilitating cattle disease. In 1889, he along with the veterinarian F.L. Kilbourne discovered Babesia bigemina, the tick-borne protozoan parasite responsible for Texas fever. This marked the first time that an arthropod had been definitively linked with the transmission of an infectious disease and presaged the eventual discovery of insects as important vectors in a number of diseases. As Dr. Francis wrote in a letter to a colleague, Dr. John Connaway of Missouri; "Dr. Salmon, Director of the Bureau of Animal Industry is calling a meeting in December in Washington D.C. to consider the present cattle fever situation in America. I am sure we will get a complete report of the work of Smith and Kilbourn, especially anything new since they published their classic work in 1893."
dc_description: Printed at bottom of photograph: "Theobald Smith in 1889."

After three years in private practice, she joined the U.S. Army Veterinary Corps. While in the Army she completed a residency in small animal surgery at Texas A&M University in 1991. She gained extensive experience with sports medicine working with the military working dogs. Dr. Yanoff retired from the Army, at the rank of Lieutenant Colonel, in 2004 after almost 21 years of service. In 2005 she joined the staff at Colonial Veterinary Hospital in Ithaca, New York, as their second surgeon. She retired from Colonial at the end of 2009 to spend more time pursuing her main interest, canine sports medicine.

dc_title: Chemistry and Veterinary Science Building at the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas, number 3
dcterms_abstract: The Chemistry and Veterinary Science Building, a two-story brick building built in 1902 and demolished in 1929, featured a progression of sections, one behind the other, with three different lengths. The facade, or first section, featured a portico, or covered entryway that sticks out from the rest of the building. Six, two-story Ionic columns supported a pediment, or triangular feature. Pairs of columns on the first floor between the larger columns supported a second-story porch. Domed corner buildings on either side of the portico gave another dimension to the building. The longest and last section featured a raised gazebo structure on the roof. According to a November 1997 Texas Aggie magazine article, the yellow-bricked building – the campus norm was red bricks – featured an early version of an air-conditioning system, but without temperature-regulating capabilities. Cushing Library was built on the same site in 1930, a year after the Chemistry and Veterinary Building was demolished.

dc_title: Man at His Desk on the Phone, number 2
dcterms_abstract: A man, thought to be Dr. Robert W. Sprowls, sits at a desk making a phone call. He started his career as a pathologist from the Texas A&M Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory and also responded to calls from animal owners and veterinarians treating both large and small animals. Dr. Sprowls has been in leadership positions at the Amarillo lab since 1975. He is nationally renowned as an astute diagnostician, helping to earn the lab's reputation as the premier U.S. facility for feedlot disease diagnostics. . His innovative approaches include promoting batch/pooled testing of clinical specimens for reproductive diseases such as Trichomoniasis. Using this method, beef producers have controlled the disease and saved 75 to 80 percent in costs over individual testing. Working with a range of clients that include some of the world's largest feedlots. He was instrumental in the development and success of the Academy of Veterinary Consultants, now a 900-member national group of beef cattle veterinarians and received the 2013 Diagnostic Services Award in Excellence from Texas A&M AgriLife.

Shows cameramen gathered with others in front of the pen where the Second Chance is held with his mother for filming. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: Three women sit at desks while Dr. Lelve Gayle stands next to the woman in the middle desk and looks at something on her desk. Dr. Gayle earned his doctorate in veterinary medicine in 1964 and Master of Science degree in veterinary toxicology in 1980, both from Texas A&M University. Prior to joining the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL) in 1976, he worked in veterinary private practice and for the U. S. Air Force. Dr. Gayle was president of the Texas Academy of Veterinary Practice in 1985 and president of the Texas Veterinary Medical Association in 1996. He worked at the TVMDL for 31 years and was executive director from 2002 until July 31, 2007 when he retired. On August 6th, 2017, Dr. Lelve Gayle passed from this life at the age of 77.

The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: Part of a History Exhibit for the College of Veterinary Medicine; Men hold cattle in front of a plank-roofed barn that was used for Texas Fever preimmunizations around 1910. This plank-roofed barn was used for Texas Fever preimmunization around 1910. "The first of the four barns was built at the "foot-hill" and was known as the "plank-roofed " barn. This barn had served the immediate need for housing the experimental animals during the early part of Francis' experiments with Texas fever. It had a roof of 1 x 12 planks, a 10-foot driveway down the middle with 10 x 10 stalls on either side and was about 60 feet long. No record has been found as to when this barn was constructed, but inquiry among graduates of the college who had classes under Francis in the late 1890's place it as early as 1895 and perhaps earlier. With many repairs and some alterations and additions, this barn survived until 1953 when it was demolished during the fourth move of the veterinary hospital to its present location west of the railroad tracks on Farm Highway 60."

Dr. Woytek, a 1965 graduate of the College of Veterinary Medicine, Texas A&M University, has earned the respect of veterinarians across the state and nation. He promotes personal, professional, and organization development while fostering loyalty among former students and friends of the College. As vice president for development with Texas A&M Foundation, and director of alumni relations for the College of Veterinary Medicine & Biomedical Sciences, Dr. Woytek assists donors in identifying the areas and programs in the college where their support will enhance Texas A&M's capability to be among the best universities. He joined the Foundation in December 1989, and his fund-raising initiatives have yielded high expectations and successful results that have benefited the College greatly.

dc_subject: Genealogy|Francis, Mark, 1863-1936--Family|Francis family
dcterms_isPartOf: College of Veterinary Medicine Image Collection

dc_rights: In copyright - educational use permitted; see http://rightsstatements.org/vocab/InC-EDU/1.0/ for more information
dcterms_abstract: A photo post card showing relatives of the Francis family. It features what appears to be a family consisting of a mother and father and three young children. These are from the estate of Dr. Mark Francis' son. The man in this photograph has a strong family resemblance to the Francis brothers. Mark Francis had four brothers; William, David, John and Edward. William a farmer, remained on the family farm and died an unmarried man with no children. David, also a farmer married a woman named Ella or Etta and they had five children; four girls and one boy. John became a local physician and practiced medicine in Hamilton, Ohio. All census records show him as single and a boarder in other homes. Edward, also a doctor who specialized in science and research lived in Washington D. C. devoting his time to the betterment of public health in our country. In 1928 he was nominated for a Nobel Prize for his work with Bacillus tularense, becoming infected and treating himself in the process; and since 1959 the tularemia organism has been known as Francisella in honor of his research. No record has been found that he was ever married or had children. It is thought that the people in this photograph could be the family of David Francis or at least a close cousin.

dc_title: Dr. James Womack Looking at Electrophoresis Results in a Lab
dcterms_abstract: Dr. James Womack examines a chart showing lab results of electrophoresis gels while furthering his work on mapping the bovine genome. During the year this photo was taken, he became the Director of the Center for Animal Genetics at the Institute of Biosciences & Technology and held this position until 1996. Dr. Womack received a B.S. in Mathematics Education from Abilene Christian College in 1964, and his Ph. D. in Genetics at Oregon State University in 1968. Dr. Womack then joined the Department of Biology at Abilene Christian College as an Assistant Professor and was promoted to Associate Professor in 1971. He joined the Jackson Laboratory as a Visiting Scientist in 1973 and became a Staff Scientist in 1975. He later joined the Texas A&M University faculty in 1977 as an Associate Professor and was promoted to Professor in 1981. From 1990 to 1993, he was named Interim Associate Department Head for the Department of Veterinary Pathobiology. Dr. Womack has been a Member of National Academy of Sciences since 1999 and received the Wolf Prize in Agriculture in 2001.

dc_title: Two Men with a Dead Calf, number 1
dcterms_abstract: Two men in a field observe a calf that has died. It is thought that the man on the left may possibly be Roy Herrmann, a rancher from Caldwell, Texas, who also served as president of the Texas Hereford Association from 1975 to 1976. The man on the right is believed to be R. W. Sprowls, a pathologist from the Texas A&M Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory who responded to calls from animal owners and to veterinarians treating both large and small animals. Starting his career as a pathologist, Dr. Sprowls has been in leadership positions at the Amarillo lab since 1975. He is nationally renowned as an astute diagnostician, helping to earn the lab's reputation as the premier U.S. facility for feedlot disease diagnostics. . His innovative approaches include promoting batch/pooled testing of clinical specimens for reproductive diseases such as Trichomoniasis. Using this method, beef producers have controlled the disease and saved 75 to 80 percent in costs over individual testing. Working with a range of clients that include some of the world's largest feedlots. He was instrumental in the development and success of the Academy of Veterinary Consultants, now a 900-member national group of beef cattle veterinarians and received the 2013 Diagnostic Services Award in Excellence from Texas A&M AgriLife.

dc_title: Two Men in a Lab, number 1
dcterms_abstract: A man with a mustache uses a Coulter Counter (M530/0078) and man in foreground of the image views a slide on a compound light microscope. A Coulter Counter is an apparatus for counting and sizing particles suspended in electrolytes, used for cells, bacteria, prokaryotic cells, and virus particles. Most often, this machine is used to count red blood cells, white blood cells, and other components in blood to diagnose diseases.

dcterms_abstract: Part of a History Exhibit for the College of Veterinary Medicine. dc_title: College of Veterinary Medicine History Exhibits, number 08
dcterms_abstract: Part of a History Exhibit for the College of Veterinary Medicine. Left is an image of a man wearing protective gear worn during the foot-and -mouth disease outbreak of the 1920's and 30's. He is standing next to a United States Bureau of Agriculture car with disinfecting equipment on its running board. Right is an image of 'Stable D' which was used for horses and was located behind the Veterinary Hospital built in 1932. Stable D was not completed and occupied until about 1933.
dcterms_provenance: The copy of the image of the man in protective gear was made from a missing original photograph taken from the Porter-Golden Foot and Mouth Collection. See image 0117 in this collection.

dcterms_abstract: Image of the cloned bull 'Second Chance's mother and the calf himself facing opposite direction of the mother. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dc_title: Robert and Mrs. Reese, Shandon, Ohio
dcterms_abstract: This original Civil War era carte de visite (CDV) photo of Robert and Mrs. Reese of Shandon, Ohio. It is noted that historically, the predominant Welsh community originally known as New London was renamed Shandon, and also referred by locals as "Paddy's Run" Ohio. It is unconfirmed if this couple is in any way a relation of the Francis family, or just an acquaintance from his hometown. Mrs. Reese's maiden name was Jones and one of the Francis daughters married into the Jones family.
dcterms_provenance: This nineteenth century photograph is an "Ebay" purchase made by librarian Trenton Boyd and donated to the Medical Sciences Library.

dc_title: Chemistry and Veterinary Science Building at the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas, number 4
dcterms_abstract: The Chemistry and Veterinary Building featured a progression of sections, one behind the other, with three different lengths. The facade, or first section, featured a portico, or covered entryway that sticks out from the rest of the building. Six, two-story Ionic columns supported a pediment, or triangular feature. Pairs of columns on the first floor between the larger columns supported a second-story porch. Domed corner buildings on either side of the portico gave another dimension to the building. The longest and last section featured a raised gazebo structure on the roof. The yellow-bricked building – the campus norm was red bricks – featured an early version of an air-conditioning system, but without temperature-regulating capabilities. Cushing Library was built on the same site in 1930 after the Chemistry and Veterinary Building was demolished.

dc_title: Beverly Wymola and Veterinarian with a Horse
dcterms_abstract: Beverly Wymola, animal owner and veterinarian, and Dr. Marla Hendricks treat a horse during a farm call that Dr. Hendricks made in Brazos County, off of Farm to Market Road 974. This horse, Angel (Dark Angel Queen), a registered Tennessee Walker, is being given an injection to numb the area where a growth is going to be removed. Dr. Marla K. Hendricks is a 1976 graduate of Texas A&M University with a Bachelor of Science degree in Wildlife and Fisheries Sciences and a 1985 graduate of Texas A&M School of Veterinary Medicine. She is residency trained in emergency and critical care medicine and opened a private practice, Crossroads Animal Clinic in 1990 in Waco Texas.

dc_title: Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory Employee, number 2
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Lelve G. Gayle between 1977 and 1978, talking on the phone at a desk while he reviews a file. Dr. Gayle earned his doctorate in veterinary medicine degree in 1964 and Master of Science degree in veterinary toxicology in 1980, both from Texas A&M University. Prior to joining the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL) in 1976, he worked in veterinary private practice and for the U. S. Air Force. Dr. Gayle was president of the Texas Academy of Veterinary Practice in 1985 and president of the Texas Veterinary Medical Association in 1996. He worked at the TVMDL for 31 years and was executive director from 2002 until July 31, 2007 when he retired. On August 6th, 2017, Dr. Lelve Gayle passed from this life at the age of 77.

dcterms_abstract: Woman evaluating data that is being collected by an SMA 12/60 dialyzer in a pathology lab. The SMA 12/60 dialyzer, an apparatus in which dialysis is carried out. consists of one or more containers for liquids separated into compartments by membranes. It is a continuous flow system that analyses the concentration of twelve constituents (calcium, inorganic phosphorus, glucose, urea nitrogen, uric acid, cholesterol, total protein, albumin, total bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, lactate dehydrogenase, and aspartate transaminase) in serum in relatively short amount of time.

dc_title: Dr. Roy B. England and an Unidentified Man Examine a Cow
dcterms_abstract: A cow is in a squeeze chute. The man on the left is Roy B. England, Associate Professor of Large animal medicine and surgery; and the man on the right holding a clip board is unnamed. Dr. Roy Belvin England (1925-2006) a Texas native from birth, entered the military after graduating High School in 1943. After release from active duty he attended Texas A&M and graduated with a B.S. degree in Agriculture in January of 1953. He was recalled to active military service during the Korean Conflict, in the Air Force Patrol Bomber Mariner squadron out of Japan. After his second release to inactive service in 1955, he and his father-in-law raised registered polled Hereford cattle on the Gill Ranch at Whon, Texas. He worked three years as a County Agent in Knox County, Texas. In 1964, he entered Veterinary School at Texas A&M University. He received his DVM in 1967 and joined the faculty of Large animal medicine and surgery where he did research and teaching. He was a retired Professor Emeritus by 1984. Dr. England, age 81, of Bryan, Texas died on October 11, 2006.

dc_title: Portrait of Abner Francis (the Father of Mark Francis)
dcterms_abstract: This is an original Civil War era carte de visite (CDV) photograph of Abner Francis of Shandon, Ohio, father of Mark Francis, the first veterinary professor at Texas A&M College. Then known as Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas. It is likely that Abner Francis traveled to Hamilton, which was located about 11 miles from New London, a predominant Welsh community also known as "Paddy's Run" and later renamed Shandon, to have his portrait taken by Eugene Brandt who was a portrait and Landscape photographer. Eugene Brandt owned the Mammoth Art Gallery, located over Peter Jacob's drug store opposite the county courthouse in Hamilton, Ohio from about 1868 until 1877. Mark Francis's father, Abner Francis was the grandson of Welsh emigrants David Francis and his wife Mary who settled in Paddy's Run in 1802. Abner married Martha Ann Vaughan.
dcterms_provenance: This nineteenth century photograph is an "Ebay" purchase made by librarian Trenton Boyd and donated to the Medical Sciences Library.

dcterms_abstract: Ralph Fisher with 'Second Chance' and pets the bull's head. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dc_title: Two Masters of Microbiology and Parasitology in the Lab, number 2
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Merry Rideout and Dr. John Jeff Mathewson in the Lab. Dr. Merry Rideout standing, and Dr. John Jeff Mathewson seated in the Lab. A 1976 graduate of Texas A&M University, Dr. Merry Rideout worked in diagnostics and taught in the microbiology department at the A&M Veterinary Teaching Hospital until 1988. Dr. Jeff Mathewson, (1949-2012) came to College Station to work on his Doctoral training. He went on to work as a microbiologist in Infectious Diseases and Clinical Microbiology at the University of Texas Medical School in Houston. He also served the City of Houston, Texas as Chief of Clinical Laboratory Services for the Department of Public Health. And later became Laboratory Director and Professor of Medicine and Disease Control at the University of Texas Medical School and School of Public Health. Dr. Mathewson's career took him to the Oklahoma State Department of Health, and then to Jefferson City, Missouri as Director of their State Public Health Laboratory. Dr. Jeff Mathewson passed away on September 28, 2012 after courageously fighting cancer.

dc_title: First Veterinary Operating Table at Texas A&M University
dcterms_abstract: An image of the "first operating table at Texas A&M," a Simplicity operating table, manufactured by Veterinary Specialty mfg. Co. of Grand Rapids, Michigan around 1910. The base rests on circular bearings enabling the operator to rotate the field of operation to the light. dcterms_abstract: Three women and a cat during a clinic visit. The woman in the center-right is Dr. Elizabeth McNiel, a 1992 graduate of Texas A&M University. She completed a small animal rotating internship at Angell Memorial Animal Hospital in Boston and a medical oncology residency program followed by a combined radiation oncology residency and PhD program at Colorado State University. Prior to joining the faculty of Cummings School and Foster Hospital for Small Animals in 2012, she served on the faculty of the University of Minnesota and Michigan State University. An active researcher, Dr. McNiel studies the molecular biology of canine and feline tumors in her laboratory at the Molecular Oncology Research Institute at the Tufts Medical Center. Her goal is to translate basic discoveries in the laboratory into clinical advances for animals and people.

dc_title: Man at His Desk on the Phone, number 3
dcterms_abstract: A man, thought to be Dr. Robert W. Sprowls, sits at a desk making a phone call. He started his career as a pathologist from the Texas A&M Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory and also responded to calls from animal owners and veterinarians treating both large and small animals. Dr. Sprowls has been in leadership positions at the Amarillo lab since 1975. He is nationally renowned as an astute diagnostician, helping to earn the lab's reputation as the premier U.S. facility for feedlot disease diagnostics. . His innovative approaches include promoting batch/pooled testing of clinical specimens for reproductive diseases such as Trichomoniasis. Using this method, beef producers have controlled the disease and saved 75 to 80 percent in costs over individual testing. Working with a range of clients that include some of the world's largest feedlots. He was instrumental in the development and success of the Academy of Veterinary Consultants, now a 900-member national group of beef cattle veterinarians and received the 2013 Diagnostic Services Award in Excellence from Texas A&M AgriLife.

dcterms_abstract: Dr. Albrecht Konrad Eugster sitting at a transmission electron microscope, circa 1970's. Albrecht Konrad Eugster began working for Texas A&M as the head of Diagnostic Microbiology for the Texas Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory (TVMDL). During his ten-year tenure in this position, he discovered several diseases new to Texas and in some cases, new to the world. Dr. Eugster was the first to isolate canine parvovirus II and published the first report on this disease in world literature. He also found several pathogenic organisms for the first time in Texas that caused several infections in animals. In 1980 he was appointed by the Texas A&M University System Board of Regents as Executive Director of TVMDl and later received the additional title of Associate Vice Chancellor for Agriculture. In 2001 Dr. Eugster retired and was appointed Director Emeritus by the Texas A&M System Board of Regents.

dc_title: Dr. Bonnie Beaver with Students in a  Computer Lab
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Bonnie Beaver instructs students in a computer lab. Dr. Bonnie Beaver was the first woman to join the faculty of the College of Veterinary Medicine. She accepted a position in the Department of Veterinary Medical Surgery and Radiology in 1969. Over her career she has made a name for herself as a leading well published Animal Behaviorist. She has been passionate about teaching veterinary students and promoting the profession. Her education consists of B.S. Veterinary Medicine, University of Minnesota 1966; D.V.M. Veterinary Medicine, University of Minnesota 1968; M.S. Veterinary Medicine and Surgery, Texas A&M University 1972; DSc (hon), University of Guelph 2015. Her scholarly interests are; normal and abnormal domestic animal behavior, human-animal interrelationships, and animal welfare.

dcterms_abstract: Image of Dr. Jonathan Hill, one of the lead researchers on this cloning project, with 'Second Chance' the cloned bull at 3 weeks old. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: A man in a white lab coat is inserting samples into a 'Auto-Auto-Gamma Scintillation Spectrometer' machine. A gamma scintillation spectrometer is used to detect and measure radioactive decay. The scintillator generates photons in response to incident radiation. Then, a photomultiplier converts those photons into an electrical signal therefore producing a series of pulses. Each pulse corresponds to the decay of a single nucleus. Liquid scintillation is simply mixing a radioactive material with a liquid scintillator material.

dc_title: College of Veterinary Medicine History Exhibits, number 02
dcterms_abstract: Image that has been hung on a wall as part of History Exhibits for the College of Veterinary Medicine, depicts the building referred to a "Old Main" with the student body in formation ready for inspection by the Corps Commander. This building was the first building to be built on the Texas A&M Campus, or then known as The Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas and has long been referred to as "Old Main". Built in 1875 it originally housed offices of College Administration, dormitories and a library. This building was destroyed in a fire in 1912 and students assisted in the demolishing of the remains using cannon fire instead of a wreaking ball, and in the end pulling it down with ropes. "We had a room about 14 x 16 feet that was on the ground floor of the Main Building (destroyed by fire in May 1912) that served as office, classroom and laboratory. At the end of the school year-June 1889-the adjoining room became vacant and was assigned to us as a classroom. In this unsuitable place we toiled for 15 years." --Mark Francis
dc_description: Text on placard depicted beneath the photograph in the exhibit: "Here in a room 14 x16, Mark Francis began his great work in 1888."

dc_title: Two Masters of Microbiology and Parasitology in the Lab, number 1
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Merry Rideout standing, and Dr. John Jeff Mathewson seated in the Lab. A 1976 graduate of Texas A&M University, Dr. Merry Rideout worked in diagnostics and taught in the microbiology department at the A&M Veterinary Teaching Hospital until 1988. Dr. Jeff Mathewson, (1949-2012) came to College Station to work on his Doctoral training. He went on to work as a microbiologist in Infectious Diseases and Clinical Microbiology at the University of Texas Medical School in Houston. He also served the City of Houston, Texas as Chief of Clinical Laboratory Services for the Department of Public Health. And later became Laboratory Director and Professor of Medicine and Disease Control at the University of Texas Medical School and School of Public Health. Dr. Mathewson's career took him to the Oklahoma State Department of Health, and then to Jefferson City, Missouri as Director of their State Public Health Laboratory. Dr. Jeff Mathewson passed away on September 28, 2012 after courageously fighting cancer.

dc_title: Two Men with a Dead Calf, number 2
dcterms_abstract: Two men in a field observe a calf that has died. It is thought that the man on the left may possibly be Roy Herrmann, a rancher from Caldwell, Texas, who served as president of the Texas Hereford Association from 1975 to 1976. The man on the right is believed to be R. W. Sprowls, a pathologist from the Texas A&M Veterinary Medical Diagnostic Laboratory who responded to calls from animal owners and veterinarians treating both large and small animals. Starting his career as a pathologist, Dr. Sprowls has been in leadership positions at the Amarillo lab since 1975. He is nationally renowned as an astute diagnostician, helping to earn the lab's reputation as the premier U.S. facility for feedlot disease diagnostics. . His innovative approaches include promoting batch/pooled testing of clinical specimens for reproductive diseases such as Trichomoniasis. Using this method, beef producers have controlled the disease and saved 75 to 80 percent in costs over individual testing. Working with a range of clients that include some of the world's largest feedlots. He was instrumental in the development and success of the Academy of Veterinary Consultants, now a 900-member national group of beef cattle veterinarians and received the 2013 Diagnostic Services Award in Excellence from Texas A&M AgriLife.

Professor Watkins received his BS in Veterinary Science in 1978 and his DVM from Kansas State University in 1980. Later in 1984 he completed his MS at Texas A&M University. As of 2017 he was part of the Equine Orthopedics team at the Large Animal Hospital. His scholarly interests include equine fracture management, intramedullary interlocking nail fixation, fracture biomechanics, biomaterials, implant design and testing, arthrodesis techniques, management of infection, and orthopedic applications of stem cell therapy.

dcterms_abstract: Ralph Fisher with 'Second Chance'. Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dcterms_abstract: A man with a mustache uses a Coulter Counter (M530/0078) and man in foreground of the image views a slide on a compound light microscope. A Coulter Counter is an apparatus for counting and sizing particles suspended in electrolytes, used for cells, bacteria, prokaryotic cells, and virus particles. Most often, this machine is used to count red blood cells, white blood cells, and other components in blood to diagnose diseases.

dcterms_abstract: A woman in a white lab coat uses a SMA 12/60 dialyzer, an apparatus in which dialysis is carried out consisting of one or more containers for liquids separated into compartments by membranes. dc_title: Man with Equipment, number 2: Dr. James E. Womack
dcterms_abstract: Dr. James Womack conducts research by analyzing specimens. Dr. James E. Womack a professor at College of Veterinary Medicine since 1973 and known for conducting pioneering research in the cattle genome. In 2001 he received the Wolf Prize in Agriculture. The late German-born inventor, diplomat, and philanthropist Ricardo Wolf established the Wolf Foundation through which the awards are granted. Five annual Wolf Prizes have been awarded every year since 1978 to scientists and artists in four out of five scientific fields in rotation: agriculture, chemistry, mathematics, medicine and physics.

dc_title: Dr. Claudia Barton Teaching with a Nine Headed Microscope
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Claudia Barton instructs students using a nine-headed microscope. Dr. Claudia Barton received her DVM in 1973 from the University of Missouri and came to Texas A&M to join the residency program in clinical pathology. In 1978 she became part of the Department of Small Animal Medicine and Surgery; her expertise in Veterinary Oncology adding much to the team. She has been recognized as an outstanding clinical educator by numerous organizations and has received faculty distinguished teaching and continuing education awards from TAMU.

dcterms_abstract: A man in a white lab coat is inserting samples into a 'Auto-Auto-Gamma Scintillation Spectrometer' machine. A gamma scintillation spectrometer is used to detect and measure radioactive decay. The scintillator generates photons in response to incident radiation. Then, a photomultiplier converts those photons into an electrical signal therefore producing a series of pulses. Each pulse corresponds to the decay of a single nucleus. Liquid scintillation is simply mixing a radioactive material with a liquid scintillator material.

Scientists Dr. Jonathan Hill and Dr. Mark Westhusin at Texas A&M University have successfully cloned what is believed to be the first calf cloned from an adult bull, which is also the oldest animal ever cloned, a 21-year-old Brahman from Ralph and Sandra Fisher of La Grange, Texas. The cloned calf 'Second Chance' not only looks just like his father, but also has his father's exact DNA. Their research could have enormous implications in the beef cattle industry and in the future applications of cloning technology.

dc_title: Two Masters of Microbiology and Parasitology in the Lab, number 3
dcterms_abstract: Dr. Merry Rideout and Dr. John Jeff Mathewson in the Lab. Dr. Merry Rideout standing, and Dr. John Jeff Mathewson seated in the Lab. A 1976 graduate of Texas A&M University, Dr. Merry Rideout worked in diagnostics and taught in the microbiology department at the A&M Veterinary Teaching Hospital until 1988. Dr. Jeff Mathewson, (1949-2012) came to College Station to work on his Doctoral training. He went on to work as a microbiologist in Infectious Diseases and Clinical Microbiology at the University of Texas Medical School in Houston. He also served the City of Houston, Texas as Chief of Clinical Laboratory Services for the Department of Public Health. And later became Laboratory Director and Professor of Medicine and Disease Control at the University of Texas Medical School and School of Public Health. Dr. Mathewson's career took him to the Oklahoma State Department of Health, and then to Jefferson City, Missouri as Director of their State Public Health Laboratory. Dr. Jeff Mathewson passed away on September 28, 2012 after courageously fighting cancer.

dc_title: Duane Kraemer, Associate Dean for Research and Graduate Programs
dcterms_abstract: A faculty photograph of the Associate Dean for Research and Graduate Programs; Dr. Duane Kraemer. Dr. Duane Carl Kraemer, "Dewey" was born 1933 and raised on a dairy farm near Reedsburg, Wisconsin, and obtained a B.S. degree in Animal Husbandry from the University of Wisconsin in 1955. He moved from Wisconsin and entered the graduate program at Texas A&M University where his mentor was; Dr. R. O. Berry, who from 1932 was the first to perform embryo transfers intra- and interspecifically in sheep and goats. Dr. Berry greatly influenced his interest in the field of embryo transfer technology and this led to his obtaining an M.S. degree in Physiology of Reproduction in 1960, a B.S. degree in Veterinary Science in 1965, a D.V.M. degree in Veterinary Medicine and a Ph.D. degree in Physiology of Reproduction in 1966 at Texas A&M University. Since 1959, his research has focused on various aspects of embryo transfer and related technologies. In 1971, he performed bovine embryo transfers that resulted in the first purebred calves to be produced by a commercial embryo transfer company. He worked for 8 years at the South-western Foundation for Research and Education in San Antonio, Texas, on the development of contraceptive agents and embryo transfer in baboons and reported the birth of the first non-human primate by embryo transfer in the journal Science in 1976. He subsequently reported the first births of cats and dogs by embryo transfer. With his students and colleagues, he has conducted pioneering research on non-domestic and endangered species. He has served in the capacity of an adjunct and clinical professor and scientist at five academic and research institutions. In 1975, Dr. Kraemer returned to Texas A&M University where he continues to serve as an active member of the Department of Veterinary Physiology and Pharmacology in the College of Veterinary Medicine (tenured in 1980), the Department of Animal Sciences, and the Faculties of Genetics and Reproductive Biology. He served as the associate Director of the Texas Agricultural Experiment Station and the Associate Dean for Research and Graduate Programs in the College of Veterinary Medicine in the Texas A&M University system. In 2006, he received the International Embryo Transfer Society's Pioneer Award. Currently, in 2017 Dr. Kraemer is a retired Senior Professor and frequently can be found at the College today.

dc_title: Chemistry and Veterinary Science Building at the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas, number 5
dcterms_abstract: The Chemistry and Veterinary Building featured a progression of sections, one behind the other, with three different lengths. The facade, or first section, featured a portico, or covered entryway that sticks out from the rest of the building. Six, two-story Ionic columns supported a pediment, or triangular feature. Pairs of columns on the first floor between the larger columns supported a second-story porch. Domed corner buildings on either side of the portico gave another dimension to the building. The longest and last section featured a raised gazebo structure on the roof. The yellow-bricked building – the campus norm was red bricks – featured an early version of an air-conditioning system, but without temperature-regulating capabilities. Cushing Library was built on the same site in 1930 after the Chemistry and Veterinary Building was demolished. This building was built in 1902 for $31,000 and was demolished in 1929. The next year the Cushing Memorial Library was built on this site.
